scenario: Team has simple if-else logic. Choosing between Literal Expression with if-then-else vs single-row Decision Table. Wonders best fit.
question: Literal Expression vs Decision Table for simple logic?

options.text: Decision Table should always be used instead of a Literal Expression regardless of logic complexity -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. Literal Expressions are compact for simple single-formula or if-then-else logic and are often simpler than an equivalent Decision Table with only one or two rules. Decision Tables are better for multiple rules and business-readable tabular layouts. For 1-2 rules, a Literal Expression is often the cleaner choice. For non-technical maintainers, Decision Tables are more accessible, but for developers with simple logic, Literal Expressions are more flexible.

Choose by readability + maintainability: the choice between a Decision Table and a Literal Expression in DMN should be guided by which representation makes the business logic clearest to maintainers. Decision Tables excel when logic involves multiple input combinations evaluated against rows of conditions and outputs. Both are valid DMN elements; the selection criterion is clarity, auditability, and how easily the business logic can be understood and modified by future maintainers.

Literal Expressions should always be used instead of Decision Tables for all DMN decision logic -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. Decision Tables provide a structured, visual representation of multi-dimensional business rules. Forcing all decision logic into Literal Expressions results in long, complex FEEL expressions that are harder to understand than an equivalent Decision Table with clear rows and columns.

Decision Tables and Literal Expressions are functionally identical in Camunda 8 and can always be used interchangeably -- this does not accurately reflect the behavior. Both are distinct DMN element types with different structural representations and use cases. A Decision Table organizes conditions and outputs in rows and columns with a configurable hit policy. A Literal Expression contains a single FEEL formula computing one output value.

a.text: Incorrect. Choosing decision body type: Literal Expression (per Set 13 Q39): Pros: Compact: single FEEL expression. Flexible: any FEEL logic. For developers: code-like. Cons: Less business-friendly: requires FEEL knowledge. Less visible structure: for complex logic. Best for: Single formulas: =amount * 0.1. Simple conditional: =if amount > 100 then "high" else "low". One-off computations. Decision Table: Pros: Business-readable: tabular. Multiple rules clear: row per rule. Hit policy explicit: documented behavior. Annotation column: per-rule explanation. Cons: More verbose for simple cases: overkill. Less flexible for complex logic: rules-based. Best for: 3+ rules. Business-readable logic. Audit clarity. Multiple inputs / outputs. Comparison: | Aspect | Literal Expression | Decision Table | |--------|-------------------|----------------| | Compactness | Very compact | Verbose for simple | | Business-readable | Less | More | | Multiple rules | Nested if-else | Each row | | Hit policy | Implicit | Explicit | | Annotations | In FEEL comments | Dedicated column | | Tool support | FEEL editor | Tabular UI | Example: same logic, different forms: Literal Expression: `feel if amount > 1000 and tier = "VIP" then 0.20 else if amount > 1000 then 0.15 else if amount > 500 then 0.10 else if tier = "VIP" then 0.10 else 0.05 Nested if-else. Decision Table: | amount | tier | discount | |--------|------|----------| | > 1000 | "VIP" | 0.20 | | > 1000 | - | 0.15 | | > 500 | - | 0.10 | | - | "VIP" | 0.10 | | - | - | 0.05 | More structured; per-rule visibility. For simple if-else (2 cases): Literal: feel if isPremium then "premium-tier" else "standard-tier" No Boxed Conditional body type in C8 Modeler (Pattern #41) -- dmn.md:23-28 confirms only 4 element types and Decision bodies are limited to "tables and literal expressions". Inline if cond then a else b (chainable via else if`) inside Literal Expression is the canonical C8 form. Or DT: | isPremium | output | |-----------|--------| | true | "premium-tier" | | false | "standard-tier" | Both work; Literal more compact. For complex business logic: Decision Table preferred: clarity wins. 15-20+ rules: definitely DT. For very dynamic logic: Literal Expression: more flexible. Decision Table: rules-bound. For audit / compliance: Decision Table: each rule documented. Regulatory clarity. For non-technical authors: Decision Table accessible. Literal Expression less so. For programmers: Literal Expression natural. Code-like. For maintainability: Decision Table when many rules: easier to update individual. Literal Expression: refactoring all-or-nothing. For testing: Both equally testable. DT easier to enumerate cases. Combined approach: Decision Table with FEEL cells: combine. Literal expressions in cells: complex logic per rule. Best of both. For Camunda 8: Both supported. Same engine evaluation. For modelling time: Decision Table: visual editing in Modeler. Literal Expression: text editor. For diff / version control: Both as XML. DT diff: per cell. Literal: single expression change. For migration / changes: DT changes: per rule. Literal changes: whole expression. Anti-patterns: Massive Literal Expression with many if-else: hard to read. DT with 1-2 rules: overkill. Best practices: Match form to complexity. Match form to audience. Document choice rationale. For the scenario (simple if-else): 2 outcomes: Literal Expression simpler. Decision Table still works but more verbose. Pick by team preference. For evolving complexity: Start with Literal. Refactor to DT when grows. For Camunda Modeler UX: DT visual editor: easier for many. Literal editor: text-based.
